column2: Deserialization behavior change with Records, `@JsonCreator` and `@JsonValue`

column1: - [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

The fix for 4724 addressed the case where a delegating @JsonCreator was being ignored in favor of the record's primary constructor. However, a similar conflict can happen with a properties @JsonCreator. The existing fix does not address this case.

The issue appeared between 2.17 and 2.18 and it exists in both the 2.x and 3.x branch.    We ran into this issue when trying to upgrade to 2.21.2 from a pre-2.17 version.    I'm hoping we can get a fix for 2.21 ( or 2.22 if that is imminent)
Here is a simple (albeit unrealistic) test case that demonstrates the issue.

The `@JsonCreator` on `Outer` expects a json object with a single boolean field named `renamed`.   However, when deserializing, the constructor is invoked as a delegating constructor, and its delegate expects a json object with a single boolean field named "innerValue".
public record Inner(@JsonProperty(required = true, value = "innerValue") boolean innerValue) {}
public static Outer fromJson(@JsonProperty(required = true, value = "renamed") boolean booleanValue) {
public void testDeserializationBug(boolean testVal) throws Exception {

The test tries to deserialize json of the form `{"renamed": true}`, which should succeed given the `@JsonCreator`.    However, the constructor is used instead, and deserialization fails because the delegate requires a field named `innerValue`.

The crux of the fix for [4724](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues/4724) was this section of code, which checks for the presence of an explicit delegating creators before adding the primary constructor to the mix:  https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/blob/3ccc490341363dc88131adfeddd651f86729fa5f/src/main/java/tools/jackson/databind/introspect/POJOPropertiesCollector.java#L703
I have a surgical fix that addresses this by also checking for explicit property creators.  I'll submit it as a PR, although there may well be a better way to fix it.

column2: Creator properties always come first in 3.x

column1: - [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.
When a class has a constructor with a certain order for a part of its properties, those properties are always serialized first. Is this intentional or is this a bug?

This makes it a bit harder to serialize XMLs where the order matters because they are validated against a schema. As soon as a constructor with at least one parameter is present, only the usage of `JsonPropertyOrder` allows to place them not at the beginning.

With Jackson 2, it was possible to rely on the order of properties in the class. Although the Java Specification describes the order as undefined, our tests have shown that it was indeed stable over the last 5+ years across multiple different Java versions.
If both `SORT_CREATOR_PROPERTIES_FIRST` and `SORT_PROPERTIES_ALPHABETICALLY` are disabled, the order returned by the JVM should matter. Creator properties shouldn't come first.
In the example code, the feature state of `SORT_CREATOR_PROPERTIES_FIRST` (disabled or enabled) doesn't change the outcome.

column2: Forward Object Id references inside Collection properties leak Builder instance (follow-up to #1496)

column1: #1496 enabled Object Ids with Builder-based deserialization for the common cases and added clear error reporting (`InvalidDefinitionException`) for forward Object Id references bound to scalar properties, where the reference would otherwise be silently resolved against the discarded `Builder` instance.

One case remains broken: **forward Object Id references that appear as items of a `Collection`-typed property**. The collection-item path does not go through `ObjectIdReferenceProperty.deserializeSetAndReturn`, so `DeserializationContext.addPendingForwardRef` is never called and the guard in `BuilderBasedDeserializer.finishBuild` (`hasPendingForwardRefsFor`) does not trip. As a result:
- The unresolved slot ends up holding the `Builder` instance instead of the built target object after resolution.
`Entity` uses `@JsonIdentityInfo` + `@JsonDeserialize(builder = EntityBuilder.class)`. After deserialization, `entities[0].refs[0]` is an `EntityBuilder` rather than the built `Entity` with id 2.
Two options, both requiring changes beyond the scope of #1496:

1. Plumb collection-item forward references through `addPendingForwardRef` so the existing `BuilderBasedDeserializer.finishBuild` guard catches them and emits the same `InvalidDefinitionException` ("Cannot resolve forward Object Id references for Builder-based type …").

2. Actually support the case: re-bind collection-item references from the builder to the built object when `updateObjectId` is called in `finishBuild`. This would likely require the collection `Referring` implementation to late-bind via the `ReadableObjectId` entry after `updateObjectId` rewrites the bound item.
Option 1 matches the existing fail-fast contract for the scalar case and is the minimum acceptable fix. Option 2 is the full solution.

When deserializing objects whose construction goes through a property-based creator – which includes every Java record, since records always use the canonical constructor – `BeanDeserializer._deserializeUsingPropertyBased` allocates a fresh `TokenBuffer` on every call. Each `TokenBuffer` allocates at least one `Segment` (an `Object[]` plus a token-type array). For workloads that stream many small records, this shows up prominently in allocation profiles.

Format-independent — reproduces with JSON, CSV, or any other backend. Since records are now the idiomatic way to model immutable DTOs in Java and unconditionally force this path, users migrating DTOs from classes-with-setters to records see a regression in allocation rate with no change in code shape.
The buffer's lifetime is bounded to _deserializeUsingPropertyBased, so reusing it (with `RecyclerPool`?) may be feasible.

column2: Add new extension point for `ObjectMapper`, `ObjectWriter`: handler `GeneratorInitializer` called immediately after `JsonGenerator` created, before used
column3: So there is need to allow registering something like:

column1: public void initialize(SerializationConfig cfg, JsonGenerator g) throws JacksonException
that can be configured on `SerializationConfig` via `ObjectMapperBuilder` or using new `ObjectWriter.with(GeneratorInitializer initializer)` method.

Method "initialize()" will need to be called immediately after construction of `JsonGenerator` and before it is used (by various `ObjectMapper` and `ObjectWriter` `.writeValue()` methods) or returned (by `ObjectMapper` and `ObjectWriter` `createGenerator()` methods).
The main use case is for XML module's initialization of things like XML declaration, but it can be useful for other format backends too.

column2: Fields not annotated with `@JsonView` aren't read nor written when using view in 3.x

column1: - [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

Before jackson 3, when a field doesn't have an annotation @JsonView, it was always included in reading and writing. With jackson 3, when a view is indicated, only the fields avec the corresponding view or its subclass will be included, and not the fields without indication.
This method will print an empty json.

Attributes and properties without any @JsonView annotation should be visible in any view. At least it was the behavior in jackson 2, and it should be possible to keep the same behavior without annotating all attributes.

column2: StdDelegatingSerializer.isEmpty() passes wrong argument to _findSerializer, causing ClassCastException with Kotlin value classes + NON_EMPTY

column1: - [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.
So that's my first bug report here, with the number of years I used your work guys, I wanted at least to say thanks. :p

So for the bug in itself it seems that in StdDelegatingSerializer.isEmpty() (line 209), _findSerializer(value, ctxt) passes the original unconverted value instead of delegateValue (the converted value). This is inconsistent with serialize() (line 175) which correctly passes delegateValue.

When _delegateSerializer is null (which happens, I am not certain to understand why yet, with Kotlin inline value classes where the Kotlin module registers a BoxConverter whose delegate is never resolved), the _findSerializer fallback is triggered. It finds a serializer for the wrong type (the original type instead of the converted type), then calls isEmpty() on that serializer with the converted value, causing a ClassCastException.
ser = _findSerializer(value, ctxt);        // finds serializer for original type (String)
return ser.isEmpty(ctxt, delegateValue);   // passes converted type (MyId) → ClassCastException
ser = _findSerializer(delegateValue, ctxt); // finds serializer for converted type (MyId)
ser.serialize(delegateValue, gen, ctxt);    // passes converted type (MyId) → works
The fix seems to be a one-word change on line 209: value → delegateValue.
value class MyId(@get:JsonValue val value: String)
data class MyEvent(val id: MyId, val name: String)
// ClassCastException: class MyId cannot be cast to class java.lang.String
1. The Kotlin module registers a StringValueClassBoxConverter for the id property (boxes String → MyId), but _delegateSerializer remains null and _delegateType is Object.
2. NON_EMPTY inclusion triggers BeanPropertyWriter to call StdDelegatingSerializer.isEmpty().
4. Since _delegateSerializer is null, _findSerializer(value, ctxt) is called with the original String value → resolves StringSerializer.
5. StringSerializer.isEmpty(ctxt, MyId("test-123")) casts MyId to String → ClassCastException.
6. Without NON_EMPTY, isEmpty() is never called and serialize() works fine (because it correctly uses _findSerializer(delegateValue, ctxt)).
isEmpty() should pass delegateValue to _findSerializer, matching the behavior of serialize().

Note: There may also be a secondary issue where _delegateSerializer is never resolved (stays null, _delegateType is Object). This could be a jackson-module-kotlin issue in how the BoxConverter is registered. However, even if the delegate is null, fixing _findSerializer to use delegateValue would make isEmpty() work correctly via the dynamic lookup path.

column2: Enum deserialization does not respect `JsonFormat.Feature.ACCEPT_CASE_INSENSITIVE_VALUES` override

column1: - [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

JsonFormat.Feature.ACCEPT_CASE_INSENSITIVE_VALUES is [documented](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-annotations/blob/a5262541fc887f0fd5047744bcfdc7eaa0f03335/src/main/java/com/fasterxml/jackson/annotation/JsonFormat.java#L374) as working for enums ("allows case-insensitive matching of (some) property values, such as Enums"), but it actually has no effect on enum deserialization.

When constructing `EnumDeserializer`, `BaseDeserializerFactory` [only checks](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/blob/02f7f1bd84f2dc668e45c8e234570ee9c0c31eb8/src/main/java/tools/jackson/databind/deser/BasicDeserializerFactory.java#L1088) `MapperFeature.ACCEPT_CASE_INSENSITIVE_ENUMS` when passing the `caseInsensitive` parameter.

Also, when searching for occurrences of `ACCEPT_CASE_INSENSITIVE_VALUES` [across all of FasterXML](https://github.com/search?q=org%3AFasterXML%20ACCEPT_CASE_INSENSITIVE_VALUES&type=code), there are only 17 occurrences. It is straightforward to verify that none of these occurrences affect enum deserialization.
As far as I can tell this has always been true since 2.10 when `ACCEPT_CASE_INSENSITIVE_VALUES` was introduced, even though it was documented from the start to work with enums.

column2: `JsonMapper` not thread-safe when using custom serializers

column1: - [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

When a `JsonMapper` is configured to serialize an object type differently in two different contexts using `@JsonSerialize`, e.g. a `java.util.Locale` should be serialized differently for property "propA" than for "property "propB", then the resulting `JsonMapper` will sometimes serialize "propB" like it should "propA", if there are multiple threads serializing in parallel using the same JsonMapper.
In our serialized JSON, Locales can be represented in two different ways:
2. In one case as the locale code string itself (e.g. `"en"` for `Locale.ENGLISH`)
To achieve this with Jackson we define a custom de-/serializer for all `Locale`s (for case 1) and specifically annotate the one place where it should be different (for case 2).

The resulting JSON is then compared against the expected result. column2: Usage of @JsonUnwrapped and @JsonCreator ignores values on deserialization

column1: When upgrading from version 2 to 3 we encounter the following problem with @JsonUnwrapped, @JsonCreator and deserializing a JSON that does not contain all attributes specified in the Objects:
Having the following classes (for convenience of this post I used inner classes):
public Signal(@JsonProperty("signalId") SignalId signalId, @JsonProperty("signalName") String signalName, @JsonProperty("unit") String unit) {
When using the ObjectMapper to deserialize JSON like in this test:
-> This test fails with "_expected: <SIGNAL_1-ID> but was: <defaultId>_"
**The difference between both tests is that the failing one does not specify the "unit" attribute.**

The general thing is that **if and only if not all attributes** for the object which should be deserialized **are given in the JSON**, all attributes of the deserialized object with an **@JsonUnwrapped annotation contain the default value** (set in the default constructor) **instead of the value defined in the JSON**.
Even if not all attributes of an object are specified in the JSON which is to be deserialized: The specified attributes are set in the objects created by the ObjectMapper.

If not all attributes of an object are specified in the JSON which is to be deserialized: The specified attributes with an @JsonUnwrapped annotation contain the value set in the default constructor instead of the value defined in the JSON.

When debugging the code its obvious that for the failing case (not all attributes are given in the JSON) **for all attributes annotated with @JsonUnwrapped first the @JsonCreator annotated constructor is called and afterwards the default constructor is called**. BUT the setSignalId(String signalId) method is not called, therefore leaving the object initialized with the default value.
Looking around in the various forums it seems that the combined usage of @JsonUnwrapped and @JonCreator is discouraged.
**Question**: Is that the intended behaviour or is that a bug? How to handle this?

column2: `DeserializationFeature.FAIL_ON_NULL_FOR_PRIMITIVES` treats absent field same as explicit `null`

column1: - [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.
While upgrading to Jackson 3 / Spring Boot 4 I noticed that `DeserializationFeature.FAIL_ON_NULL_FOR_PRIMITIVES` is now `true` by default, it defaulted to `false` in Jackson 2.
I agree that it's a good default to fail on attempt to deserialize JSON with `null` to a primitive, as primitives cannot be `null`.

But why does it fail on `absent` values? If I leave out a property for a reference field (`String`, `LocalDate` etc.) Jackson will default this field to `null` as expected, so I'd expect it would default `boolean` to `false` , `int` to `0` etc. Isn't this an unnecessary discrepancy between Java reference types and Java primitive types? Why is absent JSON fields for Java reference types defaulted but primitive fails?
private static final JsonMapper JSON_MAPPER = JsonMapper.builder()
record PrimitiveRecord(int int1, int int2, boolean boolean1, boolean boolean2) {
.as("Expect absent 'foo' to have default values (null for references)")
.as("Expect absent int1 and boolean2 have default values (0 and false)")
I'd expect `shouldDeserializeAbsentPrimitives()` to pass with Jackson 3 using default configuration as it does with Jackson 2 with default configuration.

Note also that you need to enable the FAIL_ON_UNKNOWN_PROPERTIES feature to actually manifest the failure with an exception, otherwise the value will be silently deserialized with null values for the unwrapped properties.

I personally think that a better OO approach would have been to add the two new methods `collectAllPropertyNamesTo` and `hasAnySetter` to the `ValueDeserializer` class so that unwrapping can be properly supported for all deserializer not just POJO ones.

column2: Empty JSON array overrides existing collection when using readerForUpdating

column1: - [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.
When using `ObjectMapper.readerForUpdating(...)` with an empty JSON array ( `[]` ), the empty array replaces the existing collection instead of being ignored.
I used some ObjectMapper configurations to skip null values or to transform empty arrays into null and skip the value during merge, but none of my attempts were successful.
However, none of them changed the behavior. An empty array ([]) still overrides the existing collection during readerForUpdating(...) merge instead of being ignored.
When performing a merge using readerForUpdating(...), an empty array ([]) should either:
- Be ignored (i.e., not override the existing collection), or
- Be treated as null so it can be skipped via existing Nulls.SKIP configuration, or
- Have a dedicated configuration option that allows controlling this behavior.

column2: `TokenBuffer` serialization fails when buffer contains integer encoded as String

column1: - [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

`TokenBuffer` serialization does not consider the case when buffer contains integer value encoded as `String`. After it is determined that the value is not `Integer`, `BigInteger`, `Long` or `Short`, serialization logic tries to cast it to `Number` and fails with `ClassCastException`.

I first encountered this issue when trying to serialize a `TokenBuffer` that was previously copied from `JsonParser` which pointed at a big integer value. Copying was done using `copyCurrentStructure()` which called `_copyBufferValue()` which in turn [wrote a big integer](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/blob/61a16f22f6eb132b07e6bf3277f10926919c0a52/src/main/java/tools/jackson/databind/util/TokenBuffer.java#L1336) using a "deferred" value which at that point turned out to be a `String` because it hasn't been decoded yet.

After examining `TokenBuffer` serialization logic I realized that this behaviour was not specific to big integers and it would lead to the same serialization error for any integer encoded as `String`.
First encountered in 2.19.4 but it still exists in 3.x

column2: Obtain ObjectMapper to create view-specific ObjectWriter in Jackson 3
column3: ### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

column1: I'm migrating a custom JsonSerializer from Jackson 2.x to Jackson 3 and encountered an API change that prevents me from achieving the desired behavior.
In Jackson 2, I could access the ObjectMapper inside serialize via JsonGenerator.getCodec() and then create an ObjectWriter with a specific view:
public void serialize(Object value, JsonGenerator gen, SerializationContext serializers) {
// use writer to serialize something with that view

This pattern was used to temporarily switch the active view for nested objects during serialization, as described in [jackson-annotations issue #132](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-annotations/issues/132). The goal is to serialize a property using a different view than the one currently active for the root object.

However, in Jackson 3, it appears that JsonGenerator no longer has a getCodec() method (or the concept of a codec has been removed/refactored). I cannot find a way to obtain the root ObjectMapper (or its equivalent) from the generator or the serialization context in order to construct an ObjectWriter with a specific view.

Is there a recommended way to achieve this in Jackson 3? If not, would it be possible to add a method like SerializationContext.writerWithView(Class<?> view) or provide access to the ObjectMapper (or a writer factory) from within a serializer?
Thank you for your work on Jackson!

But maybe it's worth it to use same new the declaration system to not even rely on the fact that the caller will not try to do writerWithView(NeverUseMe.class) ?
When interfacing with old systems, some have implementation where property order is expected.

In jackson 2 it could be set at class level with the annotation `JsonPropertyOrder `;  yet [the migration documentation](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson/blob/main/jackson3/MIGRATING_TO_JACKSON_3.md)  does not give any advice to address this need.

column2: JsonMapper seems to be not thread-safe when using the polymorphic `JsonTypeInfo.As.PROPERTY` definition (and `@JsonIgnoreProperties`)

column1: - [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.
There were sporadic errors with Jackson 3.x where JsonTypeInfo property definitions were missing. A test case was created to reproduce the issue.
value = ["typ"], // ignore manually set typ, it will be automatically generated by Jackson during serialization
allowSetters = true, // allows the typ to be set during deserialization
@JsonTypeInfo(use = JsonTypeInfo.Id.NAME, include = JsonTypeInfo.As.PROPERTY, property = "typ", visible = true)
@JsonTypeInfo(use = JsonTypeInfo.Id.NAME, include = JsonTypeInfo.As.EXISTING_PROPERTY, property = "typ", visible = true)
value = ["typ"], // ignore manually set typ, it will be automatically generated by Jackson during serialization
allowSetters = true, // allows the typ to be set during deserialization
@JsonTypeInfo(use = JsonTypeInfo.Id.NAME, include = JsonTypeInfo.As.PROPERTY, property = "typ", visible = true)
@JsonTypeInfo(use = JsonTypeInfo.Id.NAME, include = JsonTypeInfo.As.EXISTING_PROPERTY, property = "typ", visible = true)
Run the test several times: **sporadic errors occur.** e.g.:
Could not resolve subtype of [simple type, class com.example.Dog]: missing type id property 'typ' (for POJO property 'animals')
There are no errors in test execution with `JsonTypeInfo.As.PROPERTY` because the JsonMapper is thread-safe.
No exceptions or concurrency issues occur if `JsonTypeInfo.As.EXISTING_PROPERTY` is used instead of `JsonTypeInfo.As.PROPERTY`.

The configuration of `@JsonTypeInfo`and  `JsonTypeInfo.As.PROPERTY` is generated from [openapi kotlin codegen](https://github.com/OpenAPITools/openapi-generator/blob/master/modules/openapi-generator/src/main/resources/kotlin-client/typeInfoAnnotation.mustache)

column2: Confusing error-handling logic in `FunctionalScalarDeserializer` (Wrapped with exceptions)
column3: Follow up on Issue #4004 and PR https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/pull/5595

column1: ### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.
Long story short, **error types are confusing and sometimes misleading.**

Jackson 3.1 introduced `FunctionalScalarDeserializer` that provides a rich and extensible deserialization model, but error handling in user-provided deserializers is currently too loose, leading to loss of semantic meaning
// 3) Jackson wraps the exception as InvalidFormatException
1. User code throws an arbitrary exception (`IllegalArgumentException`)
3. Jackson wraps the failure as `InvalidFormatException` <--- why?
From a user perspective, encountering `InvalidFormatException` strongly suggests that the input value itself is invalid or malformed.
However, in this scenario the failure is entirely due to application/domain logic,
This makes it difficult for users to correctly interpret and classify errors
**Goal: Make exception wrapping from user-provided deserializers predictable**
Jackson should apply deterministic wrapping rules when exceptions are thrown from user-provided deserialization callbacks (for example,
`FunctionalScalarDeserializer`), so that the resulting exception type reflects
1. Provide guidelines on HOW user should throw error (and which type)
2. DO improve Exception handling logic within `FunctionalScalarDeserializer`
Below is my rough idea of mentioned guideline.
* A general-purpose deserializer that uses a {@link Function} or {@link BiFunction}
* to convert JSON scalar values (strings, numbers, booleans) into target type instances.
* This deserializer is primarily designed for String-based conversions but also
* supports other JSON scalar types via {@code getValueAsString()} coercion.
* Non-scalar JSON values (arrays, objects, embedded objects) are rejected.
*       thrown by user code are treated as explicit signals of invalid input values
* user code to indicate a deliberate mapping failure and will be propagated.
*   <li>Other runtime exceptions thrown by user code are considered application or
*       domain failures and may be wrapped as {@link com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.JsonMappingException}
* rather than being reported as input-format errors.
| Thrown by user code | Jackson behavior |
- Valid JSON input is not reported as format error
- User intent is preserved when signaling mapping failures
- Custom deserializer error behavior becomes consistent and predictable

column2: Rule 2 of issue #5318 is not met

column1: - [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.
If a bean has a no-args constructor, and a constructor with some parameters not related to the properties of the bean,
then this second constructor is called with null values (because the parameters are not fields of the JSON).
If this constructor then throws some exception (e.g. NullPointerException because the parameters are null), then the unmarshalling of the JSON to the bean is aborted.
If rule 2 of issue #5318 would be obeyed, constructor 2 would not be eligible, because it does not have parameters for all properties of the bean.
which is the case if the StdValueInstantiator found any constructor with some parameters,
even if they have nothing to do with the properties of the bean.
Example class that cannot be deserialized with jackson-databind 3.0.3 :
throw new NullPointerException("Should not be called");
... getters and setters for x and y
Note: if the Point class would have a third constructor, then everything is fine, because the no-args constructor is called.
This is because of rule 1 of issue #5318 .
A constructor with parameters should only be called if all the parameters correspond to properties of the bean.
Then those parameters receive values from the JSON fields.

column2: Lombok annotation @AllArgsConstructor does not work anymore with default values

column1: - [x] I searched in the [issues](https://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-databind/issues) and found nothing similar.

After migrating from the old Spring Boot setup (previously working on Spring Boot 3.5.8 + Jackson 2.x), a POJO that relies on a field initializer default value no longer gets that default when deserialized from JSON that omits that property.

When a request body omits activationStatus I expect userData.getActivationStatus() to be ActivationStatus.ACTIVE (the field initializer). Instead, the field is null unless I remove Lombok's @AllArgsConstructor (and, if I want builder support, also remove @Builder, or apply many invasive changes across services).

I tried per-class Jackson annotations such as @JsonSetter(nulls = Nulls.SKIP) and playing with spring.jackson.constructor-detector=explicit_only, but those did not fix the problem (likely because Lombok-generated constructors interact with Jackson’s constructor-detection behavior).
Spring Boot version 4.0.1 and lombok 1.18.42 Jackson 3
- Start Spring Boot app and POST JSON {} (no activationStatus) to /create.

When Lombok generates an all-args constructor (or builder that produces an all-args constructor), Jackson chooses constructor-based deserialization and passes null for the missing property. The constructor then assigns null to the field, overwriting the field initializer. The result is null instead of the expected default (ActivationStatus.ACTIVE).

If JSON omits activationStatus, Jackson should leave the field initializer in place (i.e., the object should have activationStatus = ActivationStatus.ACTIVE), or there should be a global configuration that causes Jackson to prefer property/setter-based population (or otherwise skip null constructor parameters) so that default field initializers are preserved — without requiring invasive per-class changes across many services.

column2: Add `MapperFeature.WRAPPERS_DEFAULT_TO_NULL` to allow configuring `JsonInclude.Include.NON_DEFAULT` default for primitive wrapper types to be `null`, not wrapped default of primitive

column1: So: it looks like `NON_DEFAULT` value for `Boolean` -- for example -- is `new Boolean(false)` (similar to the default of `false` for primitive `boolean`). But this does not really make sense: for non-primitive types, default should be `null`.
NOTE: this is different from Class-level "Class member Default" which compares actual property values a POJO is initialized with, using default (no-args) Constructor.
with Jackson v 2.8.2 outputs `{"value":false}` (as I expected). This however changes starting with 2.8.3: it outputs `{}` (tested with 2.8.3, 2.8.11.2 and 2.9.6).
**EDIT**: behavior will be controlled with a new `MapperFeature.WRAPPERS_DEFAULT_TO_NULL` -- planned to default to `false` in 3.1 (leaving existing 2.8.3 - 3.0.3 behavior as default).
